([/ˌsæskəˈtuːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is the largest city in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). It straddles a bend in the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") in the central region of the province. It is located along the [Trans-Canada](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway") [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway"); since its founding in 1882 as a [Temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement") colony, it has served as the cultural and economic hub of central Saskatchewan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

**Saskatoon** ([/ˌsæskəˈtuːn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is the largest city in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"). It straddles a bend in the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") in the central region of the province. It is located along the [Trans-Canada](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway") [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway"); since its founding in 1882 as a [Temperance](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement") colony, it has served as the cultural and economic hub of central Saskatchewan.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

With a [2021 census population](/wiki/Canada_2021_Census "Canada 2021 Census") of 266,141, Saskatoon is the [largest city in Saskatchewan](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Saskatchewan "List of cities in Saskatchewan"), and the [17th largest Census Metropolitan Area in Canada](/wiki/List_of_census_metropolitan_areas_and_agglomerations_in_Canada "List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada"), with a 2021 census population of 317,480.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Saskatoon is home to the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan"), the [Meewasin Valley Authority](/wiki/Meewasin_Valley_Authority "Meewasin Valley Authority")—which protects the South Saskatchewan River and provides for the city's popular riverbank park spaces—and [Wanuskewin Heritage Park](/wiki/Wanuskewin_Heritage_Park "Wanuskewin Heritage Park"), a [National Historic Site of Canada](/wiki/National_Historic_Site_of_Canada "National Historic Site of Canada") and [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") World Heritage applicant representing 6,000 years of [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") history. The [Rural Municipality of Corman Park No. 344](/wiki/Corman_Park_No._344,_Saskatchewan "Corman Park No. 344, Saskatchewan"), the most populous [rural municipality](/wiki/List_of_rural_municipalities_in_Saskatchewan "List of rural municipalities in Saskatchewan") in Saskatchewan, surrounds the city and contains many of the developments associated with it, including Wanuskewin. Saskatoon is named after the [saskatoon berry](/wiki/Amelanchier_alnifolia "Amelanchier alnifolia"), which is native to the [region](/wiki/Canadian_Cordillera "Canadian Cordillera") and is itself derived from the [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") **misâskwatômina**. The city has a significant [Indigenous](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") population and several urban [Reserves](/wiki/Indian_reserve "Indian reserve"). The city has nine river crossings and is [nicknamed](/wiki/List_of_city_nicknames_and_slogans_in_Canada#Saskatchewan "List of city nicknames and slogans in Canada") the "Paris of the Prairies" and "The City of Bridges".

Historic neighbourhoods of Saskatoon include [Nutana](/wiki/Nutana "Nutana"), [Riversdale](/wiki/Riversdale,_Saskatoon "Riversdale, Saskatoon"), and [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland,_Saskatoon "Sutherland, Saskatoon") which were separate towns before amalgamating with the town of Saskatoon and incorporating it as a city in 1906. Nutana, Riversdale, their historic main streets of [Broadway Avenue](/wiki/Broadway_Avenue_(Saskatoon) "Broadway Avenue (Saskatoon)") and [20th Street](/wiki/Riversdale,_Saskatoon#Commercial "Riversdale, Saskatoon"), as well as the [downtown core](/wiki/Central_Business_District,_Saskatoon "Central Business District, Saskatoon") and other central neighbourhoods are seeing significant reinvestment and redevelopment. [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland,_Saskatoon "Sutherland, Saskatoon") was a rail town beyond the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan") lands, annexed by the city in 1956.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name "Saskatoon" (in [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language"): *sâskwatôn* ᓵᐢᑿᑑᐣ, "Sæske'tune", "Saskatoon", or the [locatives](/wiki/Locative "Locative"): *misâskwatôminihk* ᒥᓵᐢᑿᑑᒥᓂᕽ, lit: "at the saskatoon berry", *misâskwatôminiskâhk* ᒥᓵᐢᑿᑑᒥᓂᐢᑳᕽ, "at the place of many saskatoon berries", *mînisihk* ᒦᓂᓯᕽ "at the berry") comes from the Cree inanimate noun *misâskwatômina* ᒥᓵᐢᑿᑑᒥᓇ "[saskatoon berries](/wiki/Saskatoon_berry "Saskatoon berry")", which refers to the sweet, violet-coloured [serviceberry](/wiki/Amelanchier_alnifolia "Amelanchier alnifolia") that grows in the area.[[12]](#cite_note-eb-12) However, the exact origin of the naming of the settlement is unclear. Founder [John Neilson Lake](/wiki/John_Neilson_Lake "John Neilson Lake") apparently told multiple stories, including the most famous, where in August, 1883 he was handed a handful of berries, told their name, and exclaimed, "Arise Saskatoon, Queen of the North!". It is considered most likely that the name in fact derived from one already in use: *manemesaskwatan*, or "the place where willows are cut", referring to the Cree practice of cutting saskatoon berry willows for arrow shafts.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Saskatoon](/wiki/History_of_Saskatoon "History of Saskatoon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/BarrColonists.jpg/250px-BarrColonists.jpg)](/wiki/File:BarrColonists.jpg)

[Barr](/wiki/Isaac_Barr "Isaac Barr") Colonists in Saskatoon in 1903. The settlement of Saskatoon saw an economic boom when the travelling Barr Colonists encamped around the community.

In 1882, the [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")-based Temperance Colonization Society was granted 21 [sections](/wiki/Section_(United_States_land_surveying) "Section (United States land surveying)") of land straddling the South Saskatchewan River, between what is now [Warman](/wiki/Warman,_Saskatchewan "Warman, Saskatchewan") and [Dundurn](/wiki/Dundurn,_Saskatchewan "Dundurn, Saskatchewan"). The aim of the group was to escape the liquor trade in Toronto and set up a "dry" community in the [Prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie") region. The following year settlers, led by [John Neilson Lake](/wiki/John_Neilson_Lake "John Neilson Lake"), arrived on the site of what is now "Saskatoon" and established the first permanent settlement. The settlers travelled by railway from [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") to [Moose Jaw](/wiki/Moose_Jaw "Moose Jaw") and then completed the final leg via horse-drawn cart, as the railway had yet to be completed to Saskatoon.[[14]](#cite_note-HistoryTo1914-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[![a woman standing over a busy trainyard in Saskatoon with the words "Saskatoon, the Wonder City"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Saskatoon_The_Wonder_City.jpg/250px-Saskatoon_The_Wonder_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saskatoon_The_Wonder_City.jpg)

Small booklet depicting a woman standing over a busy trainyard in Saskatoon (Source: <https://archive.org/details/prairiepromisespostcard_109>)

In 1885 the [North-West Rebellion](/wiki/North-West_Rebellion "North-West Rebellion") affected the tiny community in a variety of ways. [Chief Whitecap](/wiki/Chief_Whitecap "Chief Whitecap") and Charles Trottier passed through the present day University campus on their way to join [Louis Riel](/wiki/Louis_Riel "Louis Riel")'s armed forces at [Batoche, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Batoche,_Saskatchewan "Batoche, Saskatchewan"). Following the fighting at the [Battle of Fish Creek](/wiki/Battle_of_Fish_Creek "Battle of Fish Creek"), and the [Battle of Batoche](/wiki/Battle_of_Batoche "Battle of Batoche"), wounded Canadian soldiers convalesced at the [Marr Residence](/wiki/Marr_Residence "Marr Residence") which is today a historic site. A few died in care and were buried in the [Pioneer Cemetery](/wiki/Nutana_Pioneer_Cemetery "Nutana Pioneer Cemetery") near the Exhibition Grounds.

A town charter for the west side of the river was obtained in 1903, and this settlement adopted the name Saskatoon; the original townsite, which became a village that year, was renamed Nutana. In 1906 Saskatoon became a city with a population of 4,500, which included the communities of Saskatoon, Riversdale and Nutana. In 1955, a newly established community west of the city, [Montgomery Place](/wiki/Montgomery_Place,_Saskatoon "Montgomery Place, Saskatoon"), was [annexed](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation"), followed by the neighbouring town of [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland,_Saskatoon "Sutherland, Saskatoon") in 1956.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Saskatoon serves as a regional center for the northern prairies and for central and northern Saskatchewan.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [List of neighbourhoods in Saskatoon](/wiki/List_of_neighbourhoods_in_Saskatoon "List of neighbourhoods in Saskatoon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Parkland_near_Saskatoon.jpg/250px-Parkland_near_Saskatoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parkland_near_Saskatoon.jpg)

View of the [Aspen parkland](/wiki/Aspen_parkland "Aspen parkland") outside the city limits. The area is a [transitional biome](/wiki/Ecotone "Ecotone") between the boreal forest and prairies.

Saskatoon lies on a long belt of rich, potassic [chernozem](/wiki/Chernozem "Chernozem") in middle-southern Saskatchewan, northwest of [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"), northeast of [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), and east of [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), and is found in the [aspen parkland](/wiki/Aspen_parkland "Aspen parkland") biome. The lack of surrounding mountainous topography gives the city a relatively flat grid, though the city does sprawl over a few hills and into a few valleys. The lowest point in the city is the river, while the highest point is in the [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland,_Saskatoon "Sutherland, Saskatoon")-[College Park](/wiki/College_Park,_Saskatoon "College Park, Saskatoon") area.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Saskatoon, on a cross-section from west to east, has a general decline in elevation above sea level heading towards the river, and on the east bank of the river, the terrain is mostly level until outside the city, where it begins to decrease in elevation again.

Saskatoon is divided into east and west sides by the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River"). It is then divided into Suburban Development Areas (SDA) which are composed of neighbourhoods.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Street addresses are demarcated into north and south (for avenues aligned in those directions) and similar east and west (for streets aligned in those directions). West of the river, the demarcation line for north and south addresses is 22nd Street, while east and west are divided by Idylwyld Drive (north of 20th Street) and Avenue A (south of 20th). On the east side, in order to line up with 22nd/Idylwyld, Lorne Avenue demarcates east and west while Aird Street, a minor residential road, marks the north–south boundary, except in the [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland,_Saskatoon "Sutherland, Saskatoon") community where a separate east/west demarcation takes place with Central Avenue as the boundary (there is, however, no separate north–south divide). As a result of the unusual demarcation on the east side, few streets there actually carry a "North" or "West" designation, and only a few streets in Sutherland are demarcated "East" and "West".

A second major water feature aside from the river is the Hudson Bay Slough, a remnant of a glacier-formed body of water that at one time dominated the northern end of the city. Industrial development has resulted in most of the slough being drained, however a large remnant has been preserved off Avenue C as part of RCAF Memorial Park, and another portion remains intact within the Hudson Bay Industrial area. Several residential communities also feature "man-made lakes," with [Lakeview](/wiki/Lakeview,_Saskatoon "Lakeview, Saskatoon") (developed from the early 1980s) being the first.

[Pike Lake](/wiki/Pike_Lake_Provincial_Park "Pike Lake Provincial Park") and [Blackstrap Provincial Parks](/wiki/Blackstrap_Provincial_Park "Blackstrap Provincial Park") are both approximately 40 kilometres (25 miles) south of the city on [Highway 60](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_60 "Saskatchewan Highway 60") and [Highway 11](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_11 "Saskatchewan Highway 11"), respectively. [Batoche](/wiki/Batoche,_Saskatchewan "Batoche, Saskatchewan"), a national historic site associated with the [North-West Rebellion](/wiki/North-West_Rebellion "North-West Rebellion") of 1885, is 90 km (56 mi) north of the city. Both Blackstrap and Batoche are popular destinations for school field trips.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Saskatoon experiences a borderline [cold semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *BSk*) to [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*), with typically warm summers and long, cold winters. Climate data from the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan"), in the inner city meets semi-arid criteria. This is due to slightly higher average annual temperature and slightly lower average annual precipitation than the Airport, on the city's northwest periphery.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Saskatoon_in_Winter.jpg/250px-Saskatoon_in_Winter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saskatoon_in_Winter.jpg)

Winter skyline of Saskatoon with the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") bisecting the city. Winters in the city are long and cold.

The city has four distinct seasons and is in plant [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 3b.[[20]](#cite_note-Canadian_Plant_Hardiness_Zones-20) Saskatoon has a dry climate and sees 352.3 millimetres (13.87 inches) of precipitation per year on average, with the summer being the wettest season. Saskatoon is sunnier than average in Canada as a result, averaging 2,350.4 hours of bright sunshine annually. The extreme temperatures are typically accompanied by below average levels of humidity. Thunderstorms are common in the summer months and can be severe with torrential rain, hail, high winds, intense lightning and, on occasion, [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"). The frost-free growing season lasts from May 21 to September 15,[[21]](#cite_note-21) but due to Saskatoon's northerly location, damaging frosts have occurred as late as June 14[[22]](#cite_note-22) and again as early as August. The average daytime high temperature peaks at 25.8 °C (78.4 °F) from July 31 to August 8.[[23]](#cite_note-July_31_Almanac-23)[[24]](#cite_note-August_8_Almanac-24)

The "[Blizzard of 2007](/wiki/Saskatchewan_blizzard_of_2007 "Saskatchewan blizzard of 2007")" was described by many residents as the worst they had seen and paralyzed the city with its low visibility, extreme cold and large volume of snow.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Winds rose to over 90 km/h (56 mph) and an estimated 25 cm (9.8 in) of snow fell throughout the day.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Many area residents took refuge overnight at area work places, shopping centres, hospitals and the university.

The highest temperature ever recorded in Saskatoon was 41.5 °C (106.7 °F) on 6 June 1988.[[27]](#cite_note-Saskatoon_2-27) The lowest temperature ever recorded was −50.0 °C (−58.0 °F) on 1 February 1893.[[28]](#cite_note-climate-28)

In the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") conducted by [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada"), Saskatoon had a population of 266,141 living in 107,052 of its 115,619 total private dwellings, a change of 7.7% from its 2016 population of 247,201. The population of Saskatoon is made up of 50.9% women and 49.1% men.[[42]](#cite_note-43) With a land area of 226.56 km2 (87.48 sq mi), it had a population density of 1,174.7/km2 (3,042.5/sq mi) in 2021.[[43]](#cite_note-2021census-44)

At the [census metropolitan area](/wiki/Census_metropolitan_area "Census metropolitan area") (CMA) level in the 2021 census, the Saskatoon CMA had a population of 317,480 living in 125,100 of its 134,720 total private dwellings, a change of 7.6% from its 2016 population of 295,095. With a land area of 5,864.48 km2 (2,264.29 sq mi), it had a population density of 54.1/km2 (140.2/sq mi) in 2021.[[44]](#cite_note-2021censusCMA-45)

The [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") reported that [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada") (individuals born outside Canada) comprise 53,210 persons or 20.4% of the total population of Saskatoon. Of the total immigrant population, the top countries of origin were Philippines (11,840 persons or 22.3%), India (5,845 persons or 11.0%), China (4,695 persons or 8.8%), Pakistan (3,975 persons or 7.5%), Nigeria (2,090 persons or 3.9%), United Kingdom (2,045 persons or 3.8%), Bangladesh (1,830 persons or 3.4%), Vietnam (1,510 persons or 2.8%), United States of America (1,470 persons or 2.8%), and Iraq (1,200 persons or 2.3%).[[45]](#cite_note-2021censusB-46)

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Wanuskewin_National_Historic_Site_of_Canada.jpg/250px-Wanuskewin_National_Historic_Site_of_Canada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wanuskewin_National_Historic_Site_of_Canada.jpg)

Located outside the city limits, [Wanuskewin Heritage Park](/wiki/Wanuskewin_Heritage_Park "Wanuskewin Heritage Park") is a [National Historic Site](/wiki/National_Historic_Sites_of_Canada "National Historic Sites of Canada"), and an archeological site. The park showcases the history of the [Northern Plains](/wiki/Plain_Indians "Plain Indians") peoples in the region.

The Saskatoon area was inhabited long before any permanent settlement was established, to which the ongoing archaeological work at [Wanuskewin Heritage Park](/wiki/Wanuskewin_Heritage_Park "Wanuskewin Heritage Park") and other locations bears witness. Canada's [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") population has been increasingly urbanized, and nowhere is that more apparent than in Saskatoon, where the First Nations population increased by 382% from 1981 to 2001;[[47]](#cite_note-statscan1-48) however, a portion of this increase, possibly as much as half, is believed to be due to more people identifying themselves as Indigenous in the census rather than migration or birth rate.

Saskatoon has the second highest percentage of Indigenous population among major Canadian cities at approximately 11.5%,[[45]](#cite_note-2021censusB-46) behind [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg") at 12.4%[[48]](#cite_note-2021CityCensus-49) and [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan") close by with 10.4%;[[49]](#cite_note-2021censusRegina-50) in certain neighbourhoods such as [Pleasant Hill](/wiki/Pleasant_Hill,_Saskatoon,_Saskatchewan "Pleasant Hill, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan"), this percentage exceeds 40%.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Most First Nations residents are of [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree") or [Dakota](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") cultural background although to a lesser extent [Saulteaux](/wiki/Saulteaux "Saulteaux"), [Assiniboine](/wiki/Assiniboine_people "Assiniboine people"), and [Dene](/wiki/Dene "Dene") communities also exist.

Saskatoon also has a substantial [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_people_(Canada) "Métis people (Canada)") population and is close to the historically significant [Southbranch Settlements](/wiki/Southbranch_Settlement "Southbranch Settlement") to the north, as well as the Prairie Ronde settlement near [Dundurn, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Dundurn,_Saskatchewan "Dundurn, Saskatchewan").

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Religion")]

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census"), religious groups in Saskatoon included:[[45]](#cite_note-2021censusB-46)

* [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Canada "Christianity in Canada") (134,900 persons or 51.7%)
* [Irreligion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Canada "Irreligion in Canada") (98,575 persons or 37.8%)
* [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Canada "Islam in Canada") (12,985 persons or 5.0%)
* [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Canada "Hinduism in Canada") (5,125 persons or 2.0%)
* [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Canada "Sikhism in Canada") (3,155 persons or 1.2%)
* [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Canada "Buddhism in Canada") (1,935 persons or 0.7%)
* [Indigenous Spirituality](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the indigenous peoples of the Americas") (1,650 persons or 0.6%)
* [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_Canada "Judaism in Canada") (565 persons or 0.2%)
* Other (2,065 persons or 0.8%)

According to the 2011 Census, 66% of the population identified as [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian"), with [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") (28.5%) making up the largest denomination, followed by [United Church](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada") (10.9%), and other denominations. Others identified as [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") (2.6%), [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") (0.8%), [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") (0.7%), with traditional (indigenous) spirituality (0.5%), and with other religions. 28.5% of the population reported no religious affiliation.[[55]](#cite_note-61)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Events and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Events and festivals")]

Saskatoon's major arts venue is [TCU Place](/wiki/TCU_Place "TCU Place"), which is next to Midtown Plaza downtown. Since opening in 1967, it has hosted scores of concerts, theatrical performances, live events such as the Telemiracle [telethon](/wiki/Telethon "Telethon"), high school graduation and university [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") ceremonies, and conventions. It is also home to the [Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Saskatoon_Symphony_Orchestra "Saskatoon Symphony Orchestra"). It recently underwent a multimillion-dollar renovation to its main theatre (named in honour of former mayor and senator [Sidney Buckwold](/wiki/Sidney_Buckwold "Sidney Buckwold")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/ShakespeareOnTheSaskatchewan.jpg/250px-ShakespeareOnTheSaskatchewan.jpg)](/wiki/File:ShakespeareOnTheSaskatchewan.jpg)

[Shakespeare on the Saskatchewan](/wiki/Shakespeare_on_the_Saskatchewan "Shakespeare on the Saskatchewan") is an annual [Shakespeare in the Park](/wiki/Shakespeare_in_the_Park "Shakespeare in the Park") festival held in Saskatoon.

For rock concerts and major shows, [SaskTel Centre](/wiki/SaskTel_Centre "SaskTel Centre") is the main venue. It is Saskatchewan's
largest arena, with a capacity of 15,195 for sporting events and 14,000 for concerts. Musical acts from Saskatoon include [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"), [Kyle Riabko](/wiki/Kyle_Riabko "Kyle Riabko"), [Wide Mouth Mason](/wiki/Wide_Mouth_Mason "Wide Mouth Mason"), [The Northern Pikes](/wiki/The_Northern_Pikes "The Northern Pikes"), [The Sheepdogs](/wiki/The_Sheepdogs "The Sheepdogs"), [One Bad Son](/wiki/One_Bad_Son "One Bad Son") and [The Deep Dark Woods](/wiki/The_Deep_Dark_Woods "The Deep Dark Woods"), as well as countless others popular at both local and regional levels. The facility was the 2007 host for the [Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Awards_of_2007 "Juno Awards of 2007"), Canada's foremost music industry honours.

Saskatoon hosts many festivals and events in the summer, including the [Shakespeare on the Saskatchewan Festival](/wiki/Shakespeare_on_the_Saskatchewan "Shakespeare on the Saskatchewan"), The Great Plains Comedy Festival, the [Jazz Festival](/wiki/Sasktel_Saskatchewan_Jazz_Fest "Sasktel Saskatchewan Jazz Fest"), the [Saskatchewan Children's Festival](https://www.nutrienchildrensfestival.com/), the [Saskatoon Fringe Theatre Festival](/wiki/Saskatoon_Fringe_Theatre_Festival "Saskatoon Fringe Theatre Festival") (a showcase of alternative theatre), Saskatoon Folkfest (a cultural festival),[[56]](#cite_note-62) [Doors Open Saskatoon](/wiki/Doors_Open_Saskatoon "Doors Open Saskatoon"), Fairy Door Tours Saskatoon[[57]](#cite_note-63) and the Canada Remembers Airshow.[[58]](#cite_note-64)

For over 25 years, Saskatoon has hosted a gathering of antique automobiles, (mainly from the 1960s) that has grown into an event called "Cruise Weekend". The event is usually held on the last weekend (Friday, Saturday and Sunday) in August. Activities include a poker derby, dances, and a show 'N' shine with over 800 cars from all over western Canada. No admission is charged and everyone is free to walk around and enjoy the atmosphere.

The city's annual exhibition (now called the Saskatoon Exhibition but also known in previous years as Pioneer Days and "The Ex") is held every August at [Prairieland Park](/wiki/Prairieland_Park "Prairieland Park"). In the late 1990s, the Saskatoon Exhibition was rescheduled to August so that it no longer was in direct competition with the [Calgary Stampede](/wiki/Calgary_Stampede "Calgary Stampede"), which frequently overlapped the event.

Saskatoon's [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") community celebrates the festival of [Vaisakhi](/wiki/Vaisakhi "Vaisakhi") with a parade held in May.[[59]](#cite_note-65)

The francophone community is represented by the Fédération des francophones de Saskatoon which organizes many cultural events.

### Galleries and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Galleries and museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Remai_Modern_Art_Gallery.jpg/250px-Remai_Modern_Art_Gallery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Remai_Modern_Art_Gallery.jpg)

Established in 2017, [Remai Modern](/wiki/Remai_Modern "Remai Modern") is an art gallery located in Saskatoon's [Central Business District](/wiki/Central_Business_District,_Saskatoon "Central Business District, Saskatoon").

Art museums in Saskatoon include the [Remai Modern](/wiki/Remai_Modern "Remai Modern"), located at [River Landing](/wiki/River_Landing "River Landing"), a development along the shoreline of the South Saskatchewan River, in the Central Business District. The art museum is situated in a 11,582 m2 (124,670 sq ft) building designed by [Bruce Kuwabara](/wiki/Bruce_Kuwabara "Bruce Kuwabara"), and houses over 8,000 works in its collection.[[60]](#cite_note-66)[[61]](#cite_note-67) The design for the museum won the Award of Excellence from the Canadian Architect magazine in 2011. Pablo Picasso, Georges Braque and other 20th-century artists have a home in the Remai Modern museum.[[62]](#cite_note-68) Opened on October 21, 2017, the Remai Modern has been listed at no. 18 in the New York Times "52 places to go in 2018: A starter kit for escaping into the world."[[63]](#cite_note-69) The Remai Modern inherited the collection of the defunct [Mendel Art Gallery](/wiki/Mendel_Art_Gallery "Mendel Art Gallery"), which operated in [City Park](/wiki/City_Park,_Saskatoon "City Park, Saskatoon") from 1964 to 2015.

The Saskatchewan Craft Council Gallery is on the main floor of the Saskatchewan Craft Council building in the Broadway Avenue area. It is Saskatchewan's only public exhibition gallery dedicated to fine craft as an art form. Saskatchewan Craft Council Gallery presents seven to eight exhibitions each year. The SCC Fine Craft Boutique, located in the gallery, features the work of over 75 SCC fine craft artists.

The [Ukrainian Museum of Canada](/wiki/Ukrainian_Museum_of_Canada "Ukrainian Museum of Canada") is on the banks of the South Saskatchewan River. It was founded in 1941 by the [Ukrainian Women's Association of Canada](/w/index.php?title=Ukrainian_Women%27s_Association_of_Canada&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ukrainian Women's Association of Canada (page does not exist)").[[64]](#cite_note-:0-70)[[65]](#cite_note-:1-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/SRM-West.jpg/250px-SRM-West.jpg)](/wiki/File:SRM-West.jpg)

The [Saskatchewan Railway Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Railway_Museum "Saskatchewan Railway Museum") is a [railway museum](/wiki/Railway_museum "Railway museum") located west of the city limits.

The [Meewasin Valley Centre](/wiki/Meewasin_Valley_Authority "Meewasin Valley Authority"), in Friendship Park, has information on Saskatoon's history, the South Saskatchewan River, and the future of the Meewasin Valley.[[66]](#cite_note-MeewasinValley-72)

Saskatoon is also home of the [Saskatchewan Western Development Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Western_Development_Museum "Saskatchewan Western Development Museum"). This museum, one of four throughout the province, documents early pioneer life in Saskatchewan. It is noted for its interior recreation of a "Boom Town" main street, including one original building relocated from its original site. The [Saskatchewan Railway Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Railway_Museum "Saskatchewan Railway Museum") is just outside the city and includes displays of rolling stock and historic railway buildings from various parts of the province.

The [Forestry Farm Park and Zoo](/wiki/Forestry_Farm_Park_and_Zoo "Forestry Farm Park and Zoo") is a National Historic Site situated in the northeast region of the city. The Forestry Farm was a historic nursery (dating from 1913) responsible for growing many of the trees planted within the prairie provinces. In 1966 the nursery operations were discontinued and part of the region turned into a municipal park. The city zoo is also housed within the park and features over 80 species of animals.[[67]](#cite_note-73)
[Wanuskewin Heritage Park](http://www.wanuskewin.com/) is a National Historic Site situated five km to the north of Saskatoon. It is an [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") archaeological site and features displays, special events, and activities, recent renovations are on hold due to a lack of funds during the renovations.

### Theatres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Theatres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/PrairieWindAtPersephone.JPG/250px-PrairieWindAtPersephone.JPG)](/wiki/File:PrairieWindAtPersephone.JPG)

The [Remai Arts Centre](/wiki/Remai_Arts_Centre "Remai Arts Centre") is a [performing arts centre](/wiki/Performing_arts_centre "Performing arts centre") and home to the Saskatoon-based [theatre company](/wiki/Theatre_company "Theatre company"), the [Persephone Theatre](/wiki/Persephone_Theatre "Persephone Theatre").

Live theatre is a central, vibrant part of Saskatoon's culture. Saskatoon is host to a number of live theatre venues such as the [Persephone Theatre](/wiki/Persephone_Theatre "Persephone Theatre"), which is in the [Remai Arts Centre](/wiki/Remai_Arts_Centre "Remai Arts Centre") at River Landing in downtown Saskatoon, The Refinery and the Saskatchewan Native Theatre Company. The University of Saskatchewan also has a theatre called [Greystone Theatre](/wiki/Greystone_Theatre "Greystone Theatre").

The [Broadway Theatre](/wiki/Broadway_Theatre_(Saskatoon) "Broadway Theatre (Saskatoon)") primarily shows [arthouse films](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") – while the two-screen [Roxy Theatre](/wiki/Roxy_Theatre_(Saskatoon) "Roxy Theatre (Saskatoon)") is an "atmospheric-style" [second-run theatre](/wiki/Discount_theater "Discount theater") that reopened in 2005 after sitting unused for over a decade. The remainder of the city's theatres are [multiplexes](/wiki/Multiplex_(movie_theater) "Multiplex (movie theater)"). The only movie theatre in the [downtown](/wiki/Central_Business_District,_Saskatoon "Central Business District, Saskatoon") core is the [Scotia Bank VIP Theatre](/wiki/Cineplex_Entertainment "Cineplex Entertainment"); the [Capitol 4](/wiki/Empire_Theatres "Empire Theatres") shut down on April 3, 2008.[[68]](#cite_note-74) The city's other movie theatres are The Landmark Theatre in the new subdivision of Brighton, Rainbow Cinemas (a second-run cinema) and the Cineplex Cinemas at [The Centre](/wiki/The_Centre_(Saskatoon) "The Centre (Saskatoon)") mall on the city's east side.

Among the many movie theatres of the past that have come and gone was the Capitol Theatre, which opened in 1929 with a showing of the first [talkie](/wiki/Talkie "Talkie") to be exhibited in Saskatoon.[[69]](#cite_note-75) The Capitol closed in the early 1980s to make way for the Scotia Centre office tower; its name was transferred to the aforementioned Capitol 4 a block away.

### Other attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other attractions")]

One of the city's landmarks is the [Delta Bessborough](/wiki/Delta_Bessborough "Delta Bessborough") Hotel, known to locals as the *Bez*. Built by the [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway"), it was among the last [railway hotels](/wiki/Canada%27s_grand_railway_hotels "Canada's grand railway hotels") to be started before the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s brought their era to a close. Although the building was completed in 1932, it did not open its doors until 1935 due to the Depression. The Bessborough and the [Mendel Art Gallery](/wiki/Mendel_Art_Gallery "Mendel Art Gallery") are the only major structures on the river side of Spadina Crescent. One of the most frequently circulated photographs of Saskatoon is of the hotel framed in one of the arches of the [Broadway Bridge](/wiki/Broadway_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "Broadway Bridge (Saskatoon)").[[70]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Delta_Bessborough%2C_Spadina_Cres_E%2C_Saskatoon_%28505718%29_%2826106521286%29.jpg/250px-Delta_Bessborough%2C_Spadina_Cres_E%2C_Saskatoon_%28505718%29_%2826106521286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Delta_Bessborough,_Spadina_Cres_E,_Saskatoon_(505718)_(26106521286).jpg)

Completed in 1932, the [Delta Bessborough](/wiki/Delta_Bessborough "Delta Bessborough") is a [Canadian grand railway hotel](/wiki/Canada%27s_grand_railway_hotels "Canada's grand railway hotels") and a historic landmark in Saskatoon.

The [Meewasin Valley Trail](/wiki/Meewasin_Valley_Authority "Meewasin Valley Authority") follows the South Saskatchewan River through Saskatoon. Summer activities include cycling, jogging and walking through parks and natural areas. Cross-country skiing is popular during the winter months, along with skating in Kiwanis Memorial Park. Access points are found throughout the city with interpretive signage and washrooms along the route. There are parks throughout the Meewasin Valley, with washrooms, picnic facilities, and lookout points along the river bank.[[71]](#cite_note-77) In the winter the Meewasin Skating Rink is open free to the public; it is in Kiwanis Memorial Park beside the Delta Bessborough hotel. The outdoor rink has been open since 1980.

For years, a parcel of land west of the [Traffic Bridge](/wiki/Traffic_Bridge "Traffic Bridge"), south of 19th Street, and east of Avenue C has been the subject of on-again, off-again redevelopment plans. The site formerly held the Saskatoon Arena, a power plant, a branch of the [Royal Canadian Legion](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Legion "Royal Canadian Legion"), and the head offices of the [Saskatoon Public School Division](/wiki/Saskatoon_Public_School_Division "Saskatoon Public School Division"); all these structures have been demolished to make way for redevelopment, with plans for same dating back to the 1980s. The most recent version of the plan called River Landing is ongoing.[[72]](#cite_note-78) Calgary developer Lake Placid has proposed a 200 million dollar mega hotel/condo project to be built on the site although Lake Placid had difficulty securing financing and missed an October 30, 2009, deadline to submit a 4.5 million dollar payment for the parcel of land which seemingly killed the deal.[[73]](#cite_note-79)[[74]](#cite_note-80) On November 16, 2009, it was revealed by Lake Placid that the financing should be secure within a week.[[75]](#cite_note-81) In April 2010, Saskatoon City Council voted in favour of entering new negotiations with Lake Placid over the site.[[76]](#cite_note-82)
November 2010, Victory Majors Investments buys out Lake Placid's interest in the project and in
August 2011 of the next year proposed a major overhaul to original design which would later include building a 20-story residential and 14-story hotel towers, and an 18-story and a 13-story office tower as part of [River Landing Towers](/wiki/River_Landing_Towers "River Landing Towers") completed in 2021.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/MidtownPlaza.jpg/250px-MidtownPlaza.jpg)](/wiki/File:MidtownPlaza.jpg)

Located in Saskatoon's Central Business District, [Midtown Plaza](/wiki/Midtown_Plaza_(Saskatoon) "Midtown Plaza (Saskatoon)") is one of several shopping centres in the city.

The Saskatoon Farmers' Market and some commercial sites have also been developed. Future plans separate from Lake Placid include the development of a new art gallery to replace the Mendel Art Gallery by 2014.[[77]](#cite_note-83) Other landmarks in the city include the iconic Traffic Bridge (which was demolished in 2016 and is currently being replaced by a new structure evoking the appearance of the original), the University of Saskatchewan campus, and the large Viterra grain terminal which has dominated the western skyline of the city for decades and is large enough to be visible from [Pike Lake Provincial Park](/wiki/Pike_Lake_Provincial_Park "Pike Lake Provincial Park") 32 km away.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(April 2010)* |

Main article: [Economy of Saskatoon](/wiki/Economy_of_Saskatoon "Economy of Saskatoon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Nutrien_Tower.jpg/250px-Nutrien_Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nutrien_Tower.jpg)

World's largest potash producer Nutrien's corporate head office tower in downtown Saskatoon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Cameco2.jpg/250px-Cameco2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cameco2.jpg)

Headquarters for [Cameco](/wiki/Cameco "Cameco"), the world's largest publicly traded uranium company. Saskatoon's economy has traditionally been associated with the [primary sector of the economy](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy").

The economy of Saskatoon has been associated with [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash"), [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil") and [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") (specifically [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat")), resulting in the moniker "POW City".[[78]](#cite_note-pow-84) Various grains, livestock, oil and gas, potash, uranium, gold, diamond, coal and their spin off industries fuel the economy.[[79]](#cite_note-AHN-85)[[80]](#cite_note-GofS-86) The world's largest publicly traded [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium") company, [Cameco](/wiki/Cameco "Cameco"), and the world's largest [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash") producer, [Nutrien](/wiki/Nutrien "Nutrien"), have corporate headquarters in Saskatoon. Saskatoon is also the new home of BHP Billiton's Diamonds and Specialty Products business unit.

Nearly two-thirds of the world's recoverable potash reserves are in the Saskatoon region.[[8]](#cite_note-cityquickfacts-8) [Innovation Place](/wiki/Innovation_Place_Research_Park "Innovation Place Research Park") founded in 1980 brings together almost 150 agriculture, information technology, and environmental, life sciences and [agricultural biotechnology](/wiki/Agricultural_biotechnology "Agricultural biotechnology") industries in a [science park](/wiki/Science_park "Science park") or [technology park](/wiki/Technology_park "Technology park") setting.[[81]](#cite_note-87) Saskatoon is also home to the [Canadian Light Source](/wiki/Canadian_Light_Source "Canadian Light Source"), Canada's national [synchrotron](/wiki/Synchrotron_light_source "Synchrotron light source") facility.[[82]](#cite_note-Newest-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/CLS_from_the_air.jpg/250px-CLS_from_the_air.jpg)](/wiki/File:CLS_from_the_air.jpg)

The Canadian Light Source building from the air

Saskatoon's digital media scene is growing with start-up tech companies such as Noodlecake, Point2, Vendasta Technologies, and Zu.[[83]](#cite_note-89)

One of Saskatoon's nicknames, "Hub City", refers to its ideal central location within Canada for distribution and logistics.[[78]](#cite_note-pow-84) [Saskatoon John G. Diefenbaker International Airport](/wiki/Saskatoon_John_G._Diefenbaker_International_Airport "Saskatoon John G. Diefenbaker International Airport") with 105,620 aircraft movements in 2008 was listed as the [19th busiest airport in Canada, 12th busiest in passenger traffic](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Canada "List of the busiest airports in Canada").[[84]](#cite_note-2008move-90)[[85]](#cite_note-91)

Saskatoon is developing the South Central Business District, or block 146, which is called the [River Landing Project](/wiki/Central_Business_District,_Saskatoon#South_Downtown_River_Landing "Central Business District, Saskatoon").[[86]](#cite_note-92)[[87]](#cite_note-93)[[88]](#cite_note-94) Long range planning is underway for an expected city population of 325,000 by 2028 (2011 MXD report).[[89]](#cite_note-95)

Saskatoon was expected to see a 4.2 percent growth in gross domestic product for the year 2012.[[79]](#cite_note-AHN-85) The city saw a 3.4% growth in 2004, 5.1% increase in 2005 and a 2.8% increase in 2006. Saskatoon held Canada's No. 1 economic growth spot for Canada in 2005 according to the [Conference Board of Canada](/wiki/Conference_Board_of_Canada "Conference Board of Canada").[[90]](#cite_note-96)[[91]](#cite_note-97) The Conference Board again predicted the city would rate first for economic increase in 2012, showing a growth rate of 4.2%. The Saskatoon Regional Economic Development Authority (SREDA) has also been ranked amongst Canada's top ten economic development organizations by *Site Selection* magazine.[[92]](#cite_note-98)

From 1988 to 2016 [Hitachi Canadian Industries](/wiki/Hitachi#Discontinued_or_divested_businesses "Hitachi") operated a power equipment manufacturing plant. It was closed and assets acquired by Brandt Group in 2017.[[93]](#cite_note-99)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

### Municipal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Municipal")]

See also: [List of mayors of Saskatoon](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Saskatoon "List of mayors of Saskatoon")

Saskatoon is governed by a [City Council](/wiki/Saskatoon_City_Council "Saskatoon City Council") comprising ten councillors elected to represent ten separate wards, and a mayor elected city-wide. Historically, councillors have at times been elected at-large, and term lengths for both councillors (known as aldermen until 1991) and mayors have varied over time; council has operated on a ward-basis continuously since 1994, while both mayors and councillors have served four-year terms since 2012.[[94]](#cite_note-:2-100) Municipal elections are held in the fall. As of 2025, 209 individuals have served on the town or city council, and 29 individuals have served as mayor, with 24 of those individuals having first served as a councillor.[[94]](#cite_note-:2-100) The city's current mayor, elected in the [2024 municipal election](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_municipal_elections#Saskatoon "2024 Saskatchewan municipal elections"), is [Cynthia Block](/wiki/Cynthia_Block "Cynthia Block").[[95]](#cite_note-101)

### Provincial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Provincial")]

Saskatoon is represented in the [Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saskatchewan "Legislative Assembly of Saskatchewan") by 15 of the province's 61 [MLAs](/wiki/Member_of_the_Legislative_Assembly "Member of the Legislative Assembly"), including the riding of [Martensville-Blairmore](/wiki/Martensville-Blairmore "Martensville-Blairmore"), which contains one of the city's neighbourhoods. Following the [2024 Saskatchewan general election](/wiki/2024_Saskatchewan_general_election "2024 Saskatchewan general election") in October 2024, thirteen of the city's MLAs are members of the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_New_Democratic_Party "Saskatchewan New Democratic Party"), and two, including the MLA for Martensville-Blairmore, are members of the governing [Saskatchewan Party](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Party "Saskatchewan Party").[[96]](#cite_note-102)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Health care")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Royal_University_Hospital.jpg/250px-Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)

[Royal University Hospital](/wiki/Royal_University_Hospital "Royal University Hospital")

The [Saskatchewan Health Authority](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Health_Authority "Saskatchewan Health Authority") is responsible for health care delivery in the area. They operate four hospitals within the city boundaries, these include [Royal University Hospital](/wiki/Royal_University_Hospital "Royal University Hospital"), [Saskatoon City Hospital](/wiki/Saskatoon_City_Hospital "Saskatoon City Hospital"), and [St. Paul's Hospital (Saskatoon)](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Hospital_(Saskatoon) "St. Paul's Hospital (Saskatoon)"), as well as Jim Pattison Children's Hospital, which is located within Royal University Hospital.[[101]](#cite_note-107) Royal University Hospital is a teaching and research hospital that operates in partnership with the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan"). The health authority also operates hospitals in smaller neighbouring communities.[[102]](#cite_note-108) In addition to hospitals the health authority operates long-term care facilities, clinics and other health care services. [Jim Pattison Children's Hospital](/wiki/Jim_Pattison_Children%27s_Hospital "Jim Pattison Children's Hospital") began construction in 2014 and opened in 2019 under the auspices of what was then the [Saskatoon Health Region](/wiki/Saskatoon_Health_Region "Saskatoon Health Region"), since absorbed into the [Saskatchewan Health Authority](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Health_Authority "Saskatchewan Health Authority").[[103]](#cite_note-109)[[104]](#cite_note-110)

### Fire protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fire protection")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/2024_08_26_IMG_7029.JPG/250px-2024_08_26_IMG_7029.JPG)](/wiki/File:2024_08_26_IMG_7029.JPG)

An SFD fire engine on 22nd Street in [Riversdale](/wiki/Riversdale,_Saskatoon "Riversdale, Saskatoon")

Fire protection is provided by the [Saskatoon Fire Department](/wiki/Saskatoon_Fire_Department "Saskatoon Fire Department"). As of 2025, the department has 9 stations across the city and employs over 360 staff members. The department's main headquarters are located at Fire Hall No. 1, 125 Idylwyld Drive.[[105]](#cite_note-111) To carry out their duties, the department is equipped with 12 [fire engines](/wiki/Fire_engines "Fire engines"), two [ladder trucks](/wiki/Fire_truck "Fire truck"), one [heavy rescue vehicle](/wiki/Heavy_rescue_vehicle "Heavy rescue vehicle"), two [water tenders](/wiki/Water_tenders "Water tenders"), two [brush trucks](/wiki/Wildland_fire_engine "Wildland fire engine") and three water craft, with two rigid-hull boats and one inflatable.[[106]](#cite_note-112) The department has technical teams that specialize in [surface water rescue](/wiki/Surface_water_rescue "Surface water rescue"), [rope rescue](/wiki/Rope_rescue "Rope rescue"), [hazardous material disposal](/wiki/Hazardous_material "Hazardous material"), [rescue diving](/wiki/Public_safety_diving "Public safety diving"), [vehicle extrication](/wiki/Vehicle_extrication "Vehicle extrication"), [urban search and rescue](/wiki/Urban_search_and_rescue "Urban search and rescue") and [emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services").[[107]](#cite_note-113)

### Policing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Policing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Saskatoon_police_hq_1.jpg/250px-Saskatoon_police_hq_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saskatoon_police_hq_1.jpg)

Headquarters for the [Saskatoon Police Service](/wiki/Saskatoon_Police_Service "Saskatoon Police Service"). The service provides [municipal policing](/wiki/Municipal_police "Municipal police") for the city.

The [Saskatoon Police Service](/wiki/Saskatoon_Police_Service "Saskatoon Police Service") is the primary police service for the city of Saskatoon and holds both Municipal and Provincial Jurisdiction. The following services also have jurisdiction in Saskatoon: [Corman Park Police Service](/wiki/Rural_Municipality_of_Corman_Park_No._344#Emergency_response "Rural Municipality of Corman Park No. 344"), [Royal Canadian Mounted Police](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Mounted_Police "Royal Canadian Mounted Police"), [Canadian National Railway Police Service](/wiki/CN_Police "CN Police") and the [Canadian Pacific Railway Police Service](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway_Police_Service "Canadian Pacific Railway Police Service"). As of December 31, 2012, the SPS had 442 sworn members, 59 Special Constables, and 136 civilian positions.[[108]](#cite_note-114)

#### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Crime")]

The 2006 census crime data, released July 18, 2007, showed Saskatoon leading Canada in [violent crime](/wiki/Violent_crime "Violent crime"), with 1,606 violent crimes per 100,000 residents annually. However, crime statistics produced by the [Saskatoon Police Service](/wiki/Saskatoon_Police_Service "Saskatoon Police Service") shows that crime is on the decline. In 2010, total crimes against people went up 1.28% but total crimes against property fell by 11.75%.[[109]](#cite_note-sps2010ar-115) In 2014, Saskatoon dropped to fourth highest in Canada for its CSI after being in second place for several years. [Crime in Canada](/wiki/Crime_in_Canada "Crime in Canada") uses Crime Severity Index calculated using the crime rate and the severity of those crimes. 2019 saw a record 16 homicides reported in Saskatoon, giving the city a homicide rate of around 6.5 murders per 100,000 people.[[110]](#cite_note-116)

In the early 1990s, the Saskatoon police were found to engage in "[starlight tours](/wiki/Starlight_tours "Starlight tours")," where officers would arrest Indigenous men and drive them out of the city in the dead of winter to abandon them.[[111]](#cite_note-dispatch-117)[[112]](#cite_note-Windspeaker-118)

### Transportation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Saskatoon_Skyline_Night.jpg/250px-Saskatoon_Skyline_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saskatoon_Skyline_Night.jpg)

[Broadway Bridge](/wiki/Broadway_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "Broadway Bridge (Saskatoon)") is one of several bridges that cross over the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River").

#### Roads and bridges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Roads and bridges")]

Saskatoon is on the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway") spur of the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway"), also known as [Highway 16](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_16 "Saskatchewan Highway 16"), which connects Saskatchewan, [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), and [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). Highways [5](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_5 "Saskatchewan Highway 5"), [7](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_7 "Saskatchewan Highway 7"), [11](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_11 "Saskatchewan Highway 11"), [12](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_12 "Saskatchewan Highway 12"), [14](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_14 "Saskatchewan Highway 14"), [41](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_41 "Saskatchewan Highway 41"), [219](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_219 "Saskatchewan Highway 219"), [684](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_684 "Saskatchewan Highway 684"), and [762](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_762 "Saskatchewan Highway 762") all meet at Saskatoon, with [Highway 60](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Highway_60 "Saskatchewan Highway 60") terminating just outside the southwestern city limits. The following bridges cross the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") in Saskatoon (in order from upstream):

* [Grand Trunk Bridge](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "Grand Trunk Bridge (Saskatoon)") (rail)
* [Gordie Howe Bridge](/wiki/Circle_Drive_South_Bridge "Circle Drive South Bridge")
* [Senator Sid Buckwold Bridge](/wiki/Senator_Sid_Buckwold_Bridge "Senator Sid Buckwold Bridge")
* [Traffic Bridge](/wiki/Traffic_Bridge "Traffic Bridge")
* [Broadway Bridge](/wiki/Broadway_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "Broadway Bridge (Saskatoon)")
* [University Bridge](/wiki/University_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "University Bridge (Saskatoon)")
* [CPR Bridge](/wiki/CPR_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "CPR Bridge (Saskatoon)") (rail)
* [Circle Drive Bridge](/wiki/Circle_Drive_Bridge_(Saskatoon) "Circle Drive Bridge (Saskatoon)")
* [Chief Mistawasis Bridge](/wiki/Chief_Mistawasis_Bridge "Chief Mistawasis Bridge")

Construction of Saskatoon's [ring road](/wiki/Beltway "Beltway"), [Circle Drive](/wiki/Circle_Drive "Circle Drive"), began in the mid-1960s (after first being proposed in 1913), and was completed on July 31, 2013, with the opening of the $300 million South Circle Drive project.[[113]](#cite_note-southbridge-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/CN_Chappell_Yard_in_Saskatoon.jpg/250px-CN_Chappell_Yard_in_Saskatoon.jpg)](/wiki/File:CN_Chappell_Yard_in_Saskatoon.jpg)

[CN](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railroad "Canadian National Railroad") Chappell Yard

#### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rail")]

The [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") and the Canadian National Railway have connections to Saskatoon. Both railways operate [intermodal facilities](/wiki/Intermodal_freight_transport "Intermodal freight transport") and trans-load centres; while Canadian National Railway also operates an automotive transfer facility.[[114]](#cite_note-120) Saskatoon is a stop on [The Canadian](/wiki/The_Canadian "The Canadian") passenger transcontinental rail route operated by [Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail"). The [Saskatoon railway station](/wiki/Saskatoon_railway_station "Saskatoon railway station") is in the city's west end; it opened in the late 1960s as a replacement for Saskatoon's [original main station](/wiki/Saskatoon_station_(Canadian_Pacific_Railway) "Saskatoon station (Canadian Pacific Railway)") which was on 1st Avenue downtown—the relocation of the station sparked a major redevelopment of the downtown that included the construction of the [Midtown Plaza](/wiki/Midtown_Plaza_(Saskatoon) "Midtown Plaza (Saskatoon)"), [TCU Place](/wiki/TCU_Place "TCU Place") (originally named the Centennial Auditorium) and other developments. The many provincial transportation connections and geographic location of Saskatoon give it one of its nicknames *The Hub City*. The [Saskatchewan Railway Museum](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Railway_Museum "Saskatchewan Railway Museum") is just outside the city. In the early 2000s, talk about moving all the railways out of the city raised questions about a future [LRT](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system, but the city's then-Mayor said the population is too small.[[115]](#cite_note-121) The Canadian Pacific rails in particular run close to the city centre, with at-grade crossings with the main throughfares at 3rd Avenue/Warman Road, Idylwyld Drive, 22nd Street, Avenue H, 20th Street, and Avenue P. As of the city's *2021 Transportation Study,* there seems to be no progress on improvements to the railway network around Saskatoon.[[116]](#cite_note-122)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Air")]

[Saskatoon John G. Diefenbaker International Airport](/wiki/Saskatoon/John_G._Diefenbaker_International_Airport "Saskatoon/John G. Diefenbaker International Airport") (IATA: YXE), located in the city's northwest, provides scheduled and charter [airline](/wiki/Airline "Airline") service for the city, and is a significant hub for mining and remote locations in Northern Saskatchewan. Non-stop scheduled destinations include Calgary, Edmonton, Las Vegas, Minneapolis, Prince Albert, Regina, Toronto, Vancouver, and Winnipeg. Seasonal and Charter service is provided to [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Phoenix, Arizona](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") and [Churchill, Manitoba](/wiki/Churchill,_Manitoba "Churchill, Manitoba"). There are also flights connecting Saskatoon to smaller towns within Saskatchewan, such as [Stony Rapids](/wiki/Stony_Rapids "Stony Rapids") and [Wollaston Lake](/wiki/Wollaston_Lake "Wollaston Lake").

[Air Canada](/wiki/Air_Canada "Air Canada"), [WestJet](/wiki/WestJet "WestJet") and [Purolator Courier](/wiki/Purolator_Courier "Purolator Courier") all have cargo facilities at the airport. Passenger flights are operated between Saskatoon and other cities by Air Canada, WestJet, [Porter Airlines](/wiki/Porter_Airlines "Porter Airlines"), and [Rise Air](/wiki/Rise_Air "Rise Air").

[Saskatoon/Corman Air Park](/wiki/Saskatoon/Corman_Air_Park "Saskatoon/Corman Air Park") is a [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airport 15 km southeast of Saskatoon.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Saskatoon_110.jpg/250px-Saskatoon_110.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saskatoon_110.jpg)

The [Saskatoon John G. Diefenbaker International Airport](/wiki/Saskatoon_John_G._Diefenbaker_International_Airport "Saskatoon John G. Diefenbaker International Airport") is the [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") for the [Saskatoon metropolitan area](/wiki/Saskatoon_metropolitan_area "Saskatoon metropolitan area").

#### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Bus")]

Transit operations in Saskatoon are provided by [Saskatoon Transit](/wiki/Saskatoon_Transit "Saskatoon Transit"). The route system was revamped in 2018, creating high-frequency corridors on 22nd Street, 8th Street, and College Drive. An up-to-date schedule is posted at Saskatoon Transit Route & Schedule Adjustments.[[117]](#cite_note-123) Saskatoon was serviced by [STC](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Transportation_Company "Saskatchewan Transportation Company") for bus service connecting across the province until May 2017,[[118]](#cite_note-124) and [Greyhound Canada](/wiki/Greyhound_Canada "Greyhound Canada") for inter-provincial bus service between Manitoba and Alberta until Greyhound discontinued service on October 31, 2018.[[119]](#cite_note-125)

In July 2025, [FlixBus](/wiki/FlixBus "FlixBus") began operating daily rides between Saskatoon and other prairie hubs, including [Regina](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"), [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), and [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton").[[120]](#cite_note-126)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/CollegeBuilding-UofS.jpg/250px-CollegeBuilding-UofS.jpg)](/wiki/File:CollegeBuilding-UofS.jpg)

The [Peter MacKinnon Building](/wiki/Peter_MacKinnon_Building "Peter MacKinnon Building") at the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan"). A National Historic Site, the building represents an example of early-20th century [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") designs.

Saskatoon has a number of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") institutions:

* [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan")
* [St. Thomas More College](/wiki/St._Thomas_More_College "St. Thomas More College") is a [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") federated college of the University of Saskatchewan. Affiliated with the University of Saskatchewan are the [Lutheran Theological Seminary](/wiki/Lutheran_Theological_Seminary,_Saskatoon "Lutheran Theological Seminary, Saskatoon"), College of Emmanuel and St. Chad ([Anglican Church of Canada](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada")), and St. Andrew's College ([United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada")). All three are on the university campus.
* The [First Nations University of Canada](/wiki/First_Nations_University_of_Canada "First Nations University of Canada") – Saskatoon campus.
* [Saskatchewan Polytechnic](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Polytechnic "Saskatchewan Polytechnic")
* [Gabriel Dumont Institute](/wiki/Gabriel_Dumont_Institute "Gabriel Dumont Institute")
* [Saskatchewan Indian Institute of Technologies](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Indian_Institute_of_Technologies "Saskatchewan Indian Institute of Technologies")
* [Saskatoon Business College](/wiki/Saskatoon_Business_College "Saskatoon Business College")

Saskatoon has 78 elementary schools and 14 high schools, serving about 37,000 students. Saskatoon has three main school boards: [Saskatoon Public Schools](/wiki/Saskatoon_Public_Schools "Saskatoon Public Schools"), [Greater Saskatoon Catholic Schools](/wiki/Greater_Saskatoon_Catholic_Schools "Greater Saskatoon Catholic Schools") and the [Conseil des Écoles Fransaskoises](/wiki/Conseil_des_%C3%A9coles_fransaskoises "Conseil des écoles fransaskoises").

The western annexation of what is now called the [Blairmore SDA](/wiki/Blairmore_SDA,_Saskatoon "Blairmore SDA, Saskatoon") also brought the Yarrow Youth Farm within the city limits; operated by the Province of Saskatchewan, this was a correction facility for at-risk youth. The facility was subsequently closed in March 2015 and the land, located within the under-development [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Saskatoon "Kensington, Saskatoon") community, was put up for sale the following autumn.[[121]](#cite_note-127)

## Sports and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sports and recreation")]

Further information: [List of sports teams in Saskatoon](/wiki/List_of_sports_teams_in_Saskatoon "List of sports teams in Saskatoon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/SaskTel_Centre_-_Official_Image.jpg/250px-SaskTel_Centre_-_Official_Image.jpg)](/wiki/File:SaskTel_Centre_-_Official_Image.jpg)

[SaskTel Centre](/wiki/SaskTel_Centre "SaskTel Centre") is a multi-purpose arena, home to the [WHL](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League")'s [Saskatoon Blades](/wiki/Saskatoon_Blades "Saskatoon Blades") the [CEBL](/wiki/Canadian_Elite_Basketball_League "Canadian Elite Basketball League")'s [Saskatchewan Rattlers](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rattlers "Saskatchewan Rattlers") and the [NLL](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League")'s [Saskatchewan Rush](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rush "Saskatchewan Rush").

[Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") is a popular sport in Saskatoon and the city is home to numerous amateur teams, including the [Saskatoon Blades](/wiki/Saskatoon_Blades "Saskatoon Blades") of the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") (WHL). Saskatoon was a major league hockey city from 1921 to 1926 when the [Saskatoon Sheiks](/wiki/Saskatoon_Sheiks "Saskatoon Sheiks") played in the [Western Canada Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Canada_Hockey_League "Western Canada Hockey League"). Major professional hockey appeared poised to return to the city in 1982. [Bill Hunter](/wiki/Bill_Hunter_(ice_hockey) "Bill Hunter (ice hockey)"), a local sports promoter, attempted to purchase the [St. Louis Blues](/wiki/St._Louis_Blues "St. Louis Blues") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) and move them to Saskatoon. The city built a new arena to support the move, but the relocation was prevented by the league.[[122]](#cite_note-128) In the early 1990s, Hunter made a bid for a Saskatoon expansion NHL franchise, but ultimately failed to secure adequate funding.[[123]](#cite_note-129)

The city is home to two professional franchises, the [Saskatchewan Rush](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rush "Saskatchewan Rush") of the [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League") (NLL), and the [Saskatchewan Mamba](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Rattlers "Saskatchewan Rattlers") of the [Canadian Elite Basketball League](/wiki/Canadian_Elite_Basketball_League "Canadian Elite Basketball League") (CEBL). The Rush relocated to Saskatoon from Edmonton in 2016; already the defending NLL Champions, the Rush made it to the NLL final in its first three seasons in Saskatoon, winning in 2016 and 2018. The Mamba were a charter CEBL franchise and won the league's inaugural championship in 2019. Saskatoon is also home to the semi-professional [Saskatchewan Heat](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Heat "Saskatchewan Heat") of the [National Ringette League](/wiki/National_Ringette_League "National Ringette League") (NRL).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Griffiths_Stadium_at_Potash_Corp_Park.JPG/250px-Griffiths_Stadium_at_Potash_Corp_Park.JPG)](/wiki/File:Griffiths_Stadium_at_Potash_Corp_Park.JPG)

[Griffiths Stadium](/wiki/Griffiths_Stadium "Griffiths Stadium") is an outdoor stadium used primarily by the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan")'s varsity teams, the [Huskies](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Huskies "Saskatchewan Huskies").

[Canadian football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football") is another popular sport in Saskatoon, and the city is home to a number of successful football teams. The [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan") [Huskies](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Huskies "Saskatchewan Huskies") are one of the top University football programs in Canada, with three [Vanier Cup](/wiki/Vanier_Cup "Vanier Cup") national championships and 19 [Hardy Trophy](/wiki/Hardy_Trophy "Hardy Trophy") Canada West championships. The Huskies have made nine [Vanier Cup](/wiki/Vanier_Cup "Vanier Cup") appearances since 1990, and were the first team from outside of Ontario to host the Vanier Cup, doing so in 2006. The [Saskatoon Hilltops](/wiki/Saskatoon_Hilltops "Saskatoon Hilltops") of the [Canadian Junior Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Junior_Football_League "Canadian Junior Football League") have won 22 national junior championships throughout their history, the most in the country. The [Saskatoon Valkyries](/wiki/Saskatoon_Valkyries "Saskatoon Valkyries") are the [Western Women's Canadian Football League](/wiki/Western_Women%27s_Canadian_Football_League "Western Women's Canadian Football League")'s most successful club, having won 8 WWCFL Championships since play began in 2011.

The [Huskies](/wiki/Saskatchewan_Huskies "Saskatchewan Huskies") play [U Sports](/wiki/U_Sports "U Sports") league games at the University campus. Their facilities include [Griffiths Stadium](/wiki/Griffiths_Stadium "Griffiths Stadium"), [Merlis Belsher Place](/wiki/Merlis_Belsher_Place "Merlis Belsher Place"), and the Physical Activity Complex. The Huskies participate in twelve sports at the national level and have been most successful in football,[[124]](#cite_note-130) men's volleyball,[[125]](#cite_note-131) women's basketball, and men's and women's Track and Field.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/After_a_burnout.JPG/250px-After_a_burnout.JPG)](/wiki/File:After_a_burnout.JPG)

The [Saskatchewan International Raceway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_International_Raceway "Saskatchewan International Raceway") is a [drag racing](/wiki/Drag_racing "Drag racing") facility located outside the city limits. The facility features a ¼ mile [NHRA](/wiki/National_Hot_Rod_Association "National Hot Rod Association")-sanctioned [dragstrip](/wiki/Dragstrip "Dragstrip").

[Baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") is also popular in Saskatoon. The city has been home to a variety of amateur teams including the [Saskatoon Yellow Jackets](/wiki/Saskatoon_Yellow_Jackets "Saskatoon Yellow Jackets"), [Saskatoon Stallions](/wiki/Saskatoon_Stallions "Saskatoon Stallions"), and [Saskatoon Legends](/wiki/Saskatoon_Legends "Saskatoon Legends"). The [Saskatoon Berries](/wiki/Saskatoon_Berries_(baseball) "Saskatoon Berries (baseball)") joined the [Western Canadian Baseball League](/wiki/Western_Canadian_Baseball_League "Western Canadian Baseball League") as an expansion team for the 2024 season.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

[Saskatchewan International Raceway](/wiki/Saskatchewan_International_Raceway "Saskatchewan International Raceway") has been in operation for over 40 years and is home to 1/4 mile [IHRA](/wiki/International_Hot_Rod_Association "International Hot Rod Association") drag racing and holds racing events from May to September. North of the city lies [Wyant Group Raceway](/wiki/Wyant_Group_Raceway "Wyant Group Raceway"), a paved oval track home to local stock car racing as well as races for several different Western Canadian series. In 2009, the [NASCAR Canadian Tire Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Canadian_Tire_Series "NASCAR Canadian Tire Series") made its inaugural stop there, signalling a move to a larger profile track in Saskatoon. [Marquis Downs](/wiki/Marquis_Downs "Marquis Downs") at Prairieland Park hosted [horse racing](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") from 1969 until 2020; the owners hope to build a soccer-specific stadium on the site.[[128]](#cite_note-134)

The city is home to a number of sports and recreation centres. Saskatoon has three [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling") clubs: [Sutherland](/wiki/Sutherland_Curling_Club "Sutherland Curling Club"), [Nutana](/wiki/Nutana_Curling_Club "Nutana Curling Club"), and [CN](/wiki/CN_Curling_Club "CN Curling Club"). The city has two indoor soccer facilities under the control of the non-profit [Saskatoon Soccer Centre](/wiki/Saskatoon_Soccer_Centre "Saskatoon Soccer Centre") organization. [Lions Skatepark](/w/index.php?title=Lions_Skatepark&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lions Skatepark (page does not exist)") was built in the Riversdale area in 2003. Saskatoon is home to several golf courses and various parks which include tennis courts, ball diamonds, and soccer pitches for spring, summer, and fall use, and outdoor rinks for winter use.

## The Canadian Crown in Saskatoon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: The Canadian Crown in Saskatoon")]

Main article: [The Canadian Crown in Saskatoon](/wiki/The_Canadian_Crown_in_Saskatoon "The Canadian Crown in Saskatoon")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Royal_University_Hospital.jpg/250px-Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_University_Hospital.jpg)

The [Royal University Hospital](/wiki/Royal_University_Hospital "Royal University Hospital") is one of four institutions in Saskatoon that was [designated with royal status](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_organizations_with_royal_prefix "List of Canadian organizations with royal prefix") from the [Canadian monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"). The hospital received royal status from Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") in 1990.

Saskatoon has welcomed members of the Royal Family since 1919. [The Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") most recently visited for a gala concert at Credit Union Centre, before a live audience of 12,000 and television viewers nationwide in 2005. The Queen was presented with the key to the city on the same visit, after touring the Canadian Light Source synchrotron and greeting thousands of well-wishers on a walkabout at the [University of Saskatchewan](/wiki/University_of_Saskatchewan "University of Saskatchewan") (the Queen traditionally stayed at the on-campus residence of the President of the University of Saskatchewan when she visited the city). Sovereigns and consorts who have visited include [Edward VIII](/wiki/Edward_VIII_of_the_United_Kingdom "Edward VIII of the United Kingdom") as Prince of Wales in 1919, [King George VI](/wiki/George_VI_of_the_United_Kingdom "George VI of the United Kingdom") and [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_The_Queen_Mother "Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother") in 1939, and [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") and the [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_Philip,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh"), as Princess Elizabeth in 1951 and afterwards as Queen in 1959, 1978, 1987 and 2005.

Other members of the Royal Family who have visited include [Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon](/wiki/Princess_Margaret,_Countess_of_Snowdon "Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon") in 1980, the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") (now Charles III) in 2001, the [Princess Anne](/wiki/Princess_Anne "Princess Anne") in 1982 and (as Princess Royal) in 2004, the [Duke](/wiki/Andrew_Mountbatten-Windsor "Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor") and [Duchess of York](/wiki/Sarah_Ferguson "Sarah Ferguson") (Andrew and Sarah) in 1989, and the [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Edinburgh "Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh") in 1978. [Governors General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") and [Lieutenant Governors](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Saskatchewan "Lieutenant Governor of Saskatchewan") also pay regular visits to Saskatoon. Saskatonian [Ray Hnatyshyn](/wiki/Ray_Hnatyshyn "Ray Hnatyshyn") is credited with popularising his office as Governor General from 1990 to 1995. Saskatchewan Lieutenant Governors [Barnhart](/wiki/Gordon_Barnhart "Gordon Barnhart"), [Fedoruk](/wiki/Sylvia_Fedoruk "Sylvia Fedoruk"), McNab, Monroe, [Porteous](/wiki/George_Porteous "George Porteous") and [Worobetz](/wiki/Stephen_Worobetz "Stephen Worobetz") were all former residents of Saskatoon.

Connections to the crown include the royal namesakes of about one hundred neighbourhoods, parks, streets, schools and other places. These include [King George](/wiki/King_George,_Saskatoon "King George, Saskatoon"), [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth,_Saskatoon,_Saskatchewan "Queen Elizabeth, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan") and [Massey Place](/wiki/Massey_Place,_Saskatoon,_Saskatchewan "Massey Place, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan") neighbourhoods, and Victoria, Coronation and Princess Diana parks. It was at one time considered that Saskatoon's Broadway Bridge would be renamed George V Bridge.[[129]](#cite_note-135) Landmarks and institutions also have connections and these include the [Royal University Hospital](/wiki/Royal_University_Hospital "Royal University Hospital"), one of four royal designations in Saskatchewan. Grade schools named for royals include Ecole Victoria School, King George School, Queen Elizabeth School, Prince Philip School and Princess Alexandra School.

Existing and historic hotels with royal namesakes include the King George Hotel which has been recently converted to ultra-luxury condominiums, the King Edward Hotel, the Queen's Hotel and the Patricia Hotel. The [Hotel Bessborough](/wiki/Hotel_Bessborough "Hotel Bessborough") was named for a Canadian Governor General who visited the landmark under construction in the 1930s. The [Queen Elizabeth Power Station](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Power_Station "Queen Elizabeth Power Station") is within the city and named after Queen Elizabeth. The Prince of Wales Promenade along the [South Saskatchewan River](/wiki/South_Saskatchewan_River "South Saskatchewan River") is a focal point on the riverfront trails. In 2002, 378 Saskatoon residents were presented with Canada's [Golden Jubilee Medal](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II_Golden_Jubilee_Medal "Queen Elizabeth II Golden Jubilee Medal") by vice-regals to commemorate the fiftieth anniversary of the Queen's accession to the throne.[[130]](#cite_note-136)

## Sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saskatoon&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Sister cities")]

Saskatoon has partnered itself with three cities around the world. It has not taken on a new partnership since joining with Chernvitsi in 1991. The city says it is working on a proper policy and management framework for handling new applications as [Madison, Wisconsin](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin") applied to be a twin in 2018, but was told Saskatoon had no intention to move forward with the plan "at this time."[[131]](#cite_note-137)[[132]](#cite_note-138)
